chapters.title: Everyone deserves to be treated as humanely as possible
chapters.text:
There were about 1.5 million prisoners in the United States in 2016. That’s quite a lot, isn’t it? Yet rarely do our minds wander to the issues of the prison system and those who are incarcerated. This, of course, is perfectly natural, after all, prisons don’t usually make for the most pleasant small talk topic. Nevertheless, there are many discussions, especially in the U.S., about the need for prison reform. Is it truly such a pressing issue? After reading this summary, you’ll be able to answer that question for yourself.

The USA imprisons a much higher percentage of its population than any other country in the world.

Every detail in the following chapters is a part of Shane Bauer’s experience with the American prison system. As a diligent and highly professional reporter, Bauer took this investigation extremely seriously. Rarely are reporters and journalists allowed to see the true inner workings of American prisons and, even more so, American private prisons. Shane Bauer wanted to know the truth and, despite the risks, made a difficult decision to become a part of the prison system in order to expose it.

Shane Bauer wasn’t an obvious choice for such a challenging task if you take into account his past—in 2009, he was incarcerated. While working as a freelance reporter in the Middle East, he was arrested by the Iranian authorities and held captive in solitary confinement for 26 months. The experience was traumatizing, to say the least, but perhaps that is what motivated him to go back to prison. He knew what it was like and how damaging incarceration might be. From his correspondence with former and current prisoners in American solitary confinement, he knew that the American prison system was no better than what he had gone through back in Iran.

The four months I’d spent in solitary confinement in Iran was an eternity I will never erase from my psyche, but the abyss of isolation these prisoners lived in helped me to put my own struggle into perspective. ~ Shane Bauer

Shane Bauer was adamant about exposing and changing the system, and this is what brought him to CCA, Corrections Corporation of America. This organization was responsible for running private prisons in the States. As part of the undercover investigation, he spent four tiring months in one of Louisiana's private prisons, working as a correction officer and a low-level prison guard, making a mere $4 per hour.

In the following chapters, you’ll learn more about the workings and the harmful influences of a nightmare that is American private prison, the difficulties of Shane Bauer’s unusual experience as a prison guard, and the historical background that shaped America’s prison system.

chapters.title: Private prisons and forced labor are frequently synonymous
chapters.text:
In 1865, with the 13th Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, America proudly abolished slavery—this historical fact is splattered all over history textbooks. Why then, in 1956, a century later, were Black people still engaged in plantation labor and subjected to horrific punishments and living conditions in Southern states? The truth is, slavery didn’t disappear with a wave of a magic wand; it transformed into something more covert though no less cruel. Prison systems all over the U.S. grew out of slavery. The 13th Amendment had a rather helpful loophole that made forced labor possible; people could be forced to work as a punishment for their crimes.

After being an accepted practice for many years, slavery became a habit that many relied on. When plantation owners found themselves without free working hands to order around, their businesses fell into disarray. The loophole in the Amendment was their “salvation.”

Forced labor was much more productive and convenient for both state and private plantations, and the practice of turning plantations into prisons became fairly widespread very soon. Forced labor was presented as a highly effective method of correction and rehabilitation. Authorities claimed that lawbreakers could reevaluate their life choices through hard work and, therefore, better themselves while also working for the benefit of society and the economy.

This explanation, of course, was a pretty guise for the awful exploitation of American citizens, primarily Black ones. Prisoners were driven to work hard not because they craved redemption but because they were made to by being tortured, abused, and denied basic necessities such as sleep and food on a daily basis.

Being free obliges us to be responsible for those who are not—their well-being and their rights.

It is in these societal conditions that CCA was founded in 1983. Back then, the United States was spending $1 billion a year to build new prisons. The number of convicted citizens was rapidly rising, the building process wasn’t fast enough, and the existing prisons were a significant economic burden—something had to be done. CCA was an inspired idea that would solve the problem and bring substantial profit.

The company ran the prisons much cheaper and thus alleviated the state’s budget. Prisons became a new popular business; private businessmen ran the incarceration centers producing profit, and the state courts made sure that these private labor centers had enough prisoners to fill them.

In the perfect marriage of fiscal and tough-on-crime conservatism, the companies would fund and construct new lockups while the courts would keep them full. ~ Shane Bauer

Did you know? As of 2018, about 8% of U.S. prisoners are being held in private prisons.

chapters.title: A guard is also a prisoner
chapters.text:
Through CCA, Shane Bauer began his journey to becoming a corrections officer. At first, the experience didn’t differ from the process of applying to a regular job. You fill out a form, send in your resume, and sit through a few stressful interviews—nothing new or unusual. Getting into the training program for COs (correction officers) was surprisingly easy, in part because the CCA is in desperate need of people who would be willing to work for less than nothing in dangerous and demanding conditions. Among the multiple job offers, he chose Winn (Winnfield, Louisiana). the oldest private prison in the country.

After the initial application process came the four-week cadet training. Bauer diligently attended all of the lectures and took part in all of the practical tasks. The training at Winn, though, was anything but diligent—lecturers often missed their classes, much of the information was given as an afterthought, and virtually none of the required procedures were followed. Despite this lack of structure, the training process was surprisingly strict—lecturing officers, after all, were preparing the cadets for facing the harsh realities of prison life.

Mentally challenging work can be even more tiring and difficult than physical labor.

Being a prison guard also means being a prisoner. At Winn, guards have a 12-hour workday and are sometimes forced to work on weekends. They spend more time in prison than they do at home, and the experience transforms them in the most unpredictable ways: some become quiet and reserved, others aggressive and choleric. Not a single corrections officer left Winn as the same person that applied for a job there.

This job is more mental than it is physical. It requires the strength to follow the rules and uphold the distance between a guard and an inmate. The rules, though often unofficial, are set in stone among the prison staff and are what keeps the disastrous system from failing altogether.

The rules also dictate the norms of subordination. Though many corrections officers uphold the belief that every prisoner should have a chance to redeem and better themselves, they never let the inmates forget their place. The established order is what keeps the prison running.

chapters.title: We are shaped by our circumstances
chapters.text:
As in many U.S. prisons, Winn is significantly understaffed—there is just one officer per 176 inmates. This is much less than enough for things to run smoothly, and the prison is in disarray primarily due to the lack of working officers. Finding those willing to work in such conditions was almost as difficult as keeping the ones who were already employed.

Apart from being underpaid and often overworked, corrections officers were also putting themselves in danger—any of the inmates could attack them in prison or perhaps when they got out. Prisoners also tend to manipulate the guards by taunting, provoking, and insulting them. It is rather challenging to remain fair and humane in such conditions.

Research shows that, on average, about one-third of prison guards suffer from post-traumatic stress disorder, more than soldiers returning from Iraq and Afghanistan. ~ Shane Bauer

In 1976, psychologist Philip Zimbardo conducted the Stanford Prison Experiment. A group of students was divided into guards and prisoners. The prisoners were free to run the makeshift prison as they pleased. This experiment is the best example of how work as a prison guard can change and influence a person. Lack of restrictions and abundance of power tend to bring out the worst in people.

Zimbardo’s Stanford Prison Experiment suggests that under certain circumstances, we are all capable of harming others.

Similar changes happen to prison guards; over time, they lose themselves in the microworld that is prison. For Shane Bauer, this experience posed a dilemma—could he participate in a system that he considered damaging and harmful to society? He was going through the motions and slowly sinking into the prison routine, forgetting what it was like to be on the other side; trying to treat everyone as human as possible was taking way too much energy.

Though rules were of utmost importance, Shane Bauer’s experience taught him that enforcing the rules is not enough to keep you safe. The basic premise is prison is dangerous and scary. Being a prison guard means learning how to safely navigate this danger by carefully weighing your decision and being mindful of what you say, when and to whom you say it.

Making difficult decisions means taking responsibility for yourself and those who are influenced by your actions.

chapters.title: Private prisons mainly lack adequate healthcare, much less protection of human rights
chapters.text:
Even though being a prison guard at Winn is rather difficult and dangerous, it is a much better option than being a prisoner there. After all, guards have something that cannot be taken away from them—the freedom to leave when things become too much. Prisoners do not have the same option.

The conditions in this prison violate the norms and codes established by the ACA, American Correctional Association. However, when ACA representatives arrive at Winn for a regular check-up, they give it 99 out of possible the 100 points. Shane Bauer explains why that’s the case—they do not check what they know for sure violates the codes and do not interview any of the inmates; they make a loop around the premises and leave. None of the low-level corrections officers are interested in changing the system or conditions; it isn’t their job, and they aren’t paid enough.

Though virtually all living conditions are borderline terrible, the most dangerous is the lack of adequate healthcare. One of the ways in which CCA manages to minimize its spending is by limiting its healthcare budget. Medications and hospital treatment are unjustifiably large expenses for a for-profit company like CCA. Therefore, Winn prisoners are left mainly to their own devices when ill or traumatized, even though nearly 40% of inmates suffer from chronic illnesses and infectious diseases.

Progress or success cannot be achieved through cutting corners and bending the rules.

During his time at Winn, Shane Bauer met an elderly prisoner whose legs and fingers had been amputated. Bauer was shocked to find out that the man had been admitted to prison with all his limbs intact. During his imprisonment, he developed gangrene that spread all over his legs and fingers. Over four months, this inmate made at least nine official requests to see a professional doctor. All of them were denied. The staff’s neglect cost this man his legs and most of his fingers. This case isn’t an exception; it is just one of the horrible examples.

Everyone deserves a chance at redemption, yes, but most of all, everyone deserves fundamental human rights, and many American private prisons fail to ensure these rights.

A person doesn't have to be good or valuable to society to deserve respect and consideration.

chapters.title: Profit over people
chapters.text:
The main problem with CCA and American private prisons is that they prioritize economic gain over the safety and well-being of the prisoners. It seems that as long as there are profits, no one cares how the inmates turn out, but prison cannot be run as a business. One might also argue that the community should never derive profit from crimes, be it from prisoners’ forced labor or CCA’s savings.

The current private prison system, upon closer inspection, resembles the exploitative and harmful practices that were common over a century ago in the 1880s. Back then, imprisonment and slavery were basically interchangeable terms, and a convict was more likely to die or be killed than a slave. The issues stem from the same problem—profit is prioritized over everything else.

Profit can and should be a goal, but never an end in itself.

CCA acknowledges that its corrections centers have their problems but fails to take the appropriate measures to deal with them, ignoring the complaints of both guards and prisoners. Ineffective prison management combined with the lack of proper priorities makes prisons like Winn a national disaster.

They convince themselves, with remarkable ease, that they are in the business of punishment because it makes the world better, not because it makes them rich. ~ Shane Bauer

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Four months inside the prison were almost unbearable to Shane Bauer, who was still partly trying to cope with PTSD from being imprisoned in Iran. At the end of the investigation, he felt like he was losing his mind while constantly comparing Winn and Iran, the isolation he had felt, and the isolation Winn’s inmates experienced because of him. It was unbearable and, after notifying his colleagues, he decided to quit.

Inside me there is a prison guard and a former prisoner and they are fighting with each other, and I want them to stop. ~ Shane Bauer

Why should we care about the conditions in which prisoners are kept? They have committed crimes, hurt other people in different ways; don’t they deserve to suffer? For sure, if you do wrong, you need to pay for it. What matters here is the extent of how far the punishment should go. Scandinavian countries, for example, believe that significantly limiting a criminal’s freedom is already harsh enough; in some other countries, prisoners are stocked like goods in a stuffy warehouse with little to no personal space. And yes, we need to care about that because dehumanizing conditions change people forever, and the truth is that not for the better. What if someone was sentenced by mistake and has to endure?

The extensive report that Shane Bauer released after this investigation shook up the United States. The federal government declared private prisons more violent and less effective than public ones and stopped contracting federal private prisons. As a result, CCA laid off many of its prisons, including Winn. Because of one reporter and his work, everyone seemed to finally realize that the prison reform is long overdue and of utmost importance.

Try this
• To better understand the issue, look through the published photos by Shane Bauer taken during his undercover investigation.
• Spread the awareness of the problems of the prison system.
